items.title: JVol Rust
items.text:
Lightning-fast JPEG-like compression for 3D medical images, written in Rust.

JVol Rust is a high-performance reimplementation of JVol that compresses 3D medical images (NIfTI volumes) using a JPEG-inspired algorithm adapted for volumetric data.

items.title: Features
items.text:
• Fast — 6.5× faster encoding and 2.5× faster decoding than the Python implementation
  • Parallel — leverages all CPU cores via Rayon for block-level DCT computation
  • Compact — achieves ~49× compression on typical brain MRI volumes
  • Simple CLI — encode and decode with a single command
  • NIfTI support — reads .nii and .nii.gz files directly

items.title: Quick start
items.text:
# Encode a NIfTI volume to .jvol
jvol-rust encode brain.nii.gz brain.jvol --quality 60 --block-size 8

# Decode back to NIfTI
jvol-rust decode brain.jvol brain_decoded.nii.gz

items.title: Benchmark results
items.text:
Tested on the Colin27 1998 T1 brain MRI (181 × 217 × 181 voxels):

Python Rust Speedup Encode 0.686s 0.106s 6.5× Decode 0.193s 0.076s 2.5×

See the Benchmark page for full methodology and results.

items.title: How it works
items.text:
The algorithm applies a 3D variant of the JPEG compression pipeline:

  1. Pad the volume so each dimension is divisible by the block size
  2. Split into non-overlapping 3D blocks (e.g., 8×8×8)
  3. 3D DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform) on each block
  4. Quantize the DCT coefficients using a quality-dependent table
  5. Zigzag scan coefficients by distance from origin (DC → high-frequency AC)
  6. Run-length encode the DC and AC sequences separately
  7. Serialize with metadata (affine, dtype, intercept, slope)

Read more in the Algorithm page.

items.title: Algorithm
items.text: JVol Rust implements a 3D variant of the JPEG compression algorithm, adapted for volumetric medical images. This page describes each stage of the pipeline.

items.title: Overview
items.text:
The compression pipeline follows the classic JPEG approach, extended from 2D to 3D:

┌─────────┐   ┌────────┐   ┌───────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌────────┐   ┌─────┐
│  Input  │──▶│  Pad   │──▶│ Split │──▶│ 3D DCT  │──▶│Quantize│──▶│ RLE │
│ Volume  │   │ Array  │   │Blocks │   │(per blk) │   │        │   │     │
└─────────┘   └────────┘   └───────┘   └──────────┘   └────────┘   └─────┘

items.title: 1. Intensity normalization
items.text:
The input array is linearly rescaled to the range [-128, 127]:

normalized = (value - min) / (max - min) × 255 - 128

The original min (intercept) and max - min (slope) are stored as metadata for lossless reversal during decoding.

items.title: 2. Padding
items.text: The volume is zero-padded so that each spatial dimension is divisible by the block size. For example, a 181 × 217 × 181 volume with block size 8 is padded to 184 × 224 × 184.

items.title: 3. Block splitting
items.text: The padded volume is split into non-overlapping cubic blocks. With block size 8, each block is 8 × 8 × 8 = 512 voxels.

items.title: 4. 3D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)
items.text:
A separable 3D DCT-II is applied to each block independently. "Separable" means the 3D transform is decomposed into three sequential 1D DCTs along each axis:

  1. DCT along the k-axis (innermost, contiguous in memory)
  2. DCT along the j-axis
  3. DCT along the i-axis

This is mathematically equivalent to the full 3D DCT but much more efficient. The implementation uses the rustdct crate for optimized 1D DCT computation.

Parallelism

Blocks are processed in parallel using Rayon. DCT plans are pre-computed once and shared across threads via Arc, avoiding redundant FFT planning overhead.

items.title: 5. Quantization
items.text:
Each DCT coefficient is divided by the corresponding value in a quantization table and rounded to the nearest integer:

quantized[i][j][k] = round(dct[i][j][k] / qtable[i][j][k])

The quantization table is generated based on the L2 distance of each frequency index from the origin (DC component at [0, 0, 0]). Higher frequencies get larger divisors, causing more aggressive rounding — this is where the lossy compression happens.

The quality parameter (1–100) controls a multiplier on the quantization table:

  • Quality 1: very aggressive quantization → small file, low fidelity
  • Quality 100: minimal quantization → large file, high fidelity

items.title: 6. Zigzag scan
items.text:
The 3D block indices are sorted by their L2 distance from the origin [0, 0, 0]. This ordering ensures that low-frequency (high-energy) coefficients come first, followed by high-frequency (near-zero) coefficients — maximizing RLE effectiveness.

The DC component (index [0, 0, 0]) is always first.

items.title: 7. Sequence separation
items.text:
After scanning, the coefficients are split into two sequences:

  • DC sequence: one value per block (the [0, 0, 0] coefficient)
  • AC sequence: all remaining coefficients in scan order

items.title: 8. Run-length encoding (RLE)
items.text: Both the DC and AC sequences are run-length encoded: consecutive identical values are stored as (value, count) pairs. Since quantization produces many zeros, especially in high-frequency AC coefficients, RLE achieves significant compression.

items.title: Decoding
items.text:
Decoding reverses each step:

  1. RLE decode — expand (value, count) pairs back into full sequences
  2. Sequence to blocks — reconstruct 3D blocks from DC + AC sequences
  3. Inverse quantization — multiply by the quantization table
  4. 3D inverse DCT (DCT-III) — transform back to spatial domain
  5. Reassemble — place blocks back into the full volume grid
  6. Rescale — undo the intensity normalization using stored intercept and slope
  7. Crop — remove padding to restore the original volume shape
  8. Clip — clamp values to the valid range for the original data type

items.title: File format
items.text:
The .jvol file is a gzip-compressed bincode archive containing:

Field Type Description shape [u64; 3] Original volume dimensions ijk_to_ras [[f64; 4]; 4] Affine transformation matrix dtype enum Original data type (U8, I16, F32, etc.) intercept f64 Minimum intensity value slope f64 Intensity range (max - min) block_shape [u64; 3] Block dimensions quality u8 Quality parameter used for encoding quantization_table Vec<f32> Flattened quantization table dc_rle RLE data Run-length encoded DC coefficients ac_rle RLE data Run-length encoded AC coefficients

items.title: Benchmark
items.text: Comparison of the Python JVol implementation against this Rust implementation.

items.title: Setup
items.text:
• Test data: Colin27 1998 T1 brain MRI (colin27_t1_tal_lin.nii.gz)
  • Volume shape: 181 × 217 × 181 voxels
  • File size: 24.1 MB (gzip-compressed NIfTI)
  • Parameters: quality = 60, block size = 8
  • Runs: 5 iterations, median reported
  • Hardware: Apple Silicon (M-series)

items.title: Results
items.text:
Python Rust Speedup Encode 0.686 s 0.106 s 6.5× Decode 0.193 s 0.076 s 2.5×

What's measured

  • Rust times measure the core algorithm only (no NIfTI file I/O)
  • Python times include .npz file I/O but not NIfTI I/O
  • Both exclude the time to load/write NIfTI files for fair comparison

items.title: Key optimizations in the Rust implementation
items.text:
1. Pre-planned DCT — DCT plans are computed once and shared across threads via Arc, eliminating per-block FFT planning overhead
  2. Rayon parallelism — all blocks are processed in parallel for both encoding (DCT + quantization) and decoding (inverse DCT + dequantization)
  3. Fused passes — DCT and quantization are combined in a single parallel pass, reducing memory allocations
  4. Release-mode LTO — link-time optimization enabled for maximum single-threaded performance

items.title: Reproducing
items.text:
Run the benchmark script from the repository root:

uv run benchmark.py

This requires:

  • The Rust binary built in release mode (cargo build --release)
  • The Colin27 1998 T1 NIfTI file at ~/.cache/torchio/mni_colin27_1998_nifti/colin27_t1_tal_lin.nii.gz

The script will install the Python jvol package automatically via uv.

items.title: CLI Reference
items.text: JVol Rust provides three subcommands: encode, decode, and bench.

items.title: jvol-rust encode
items.text:
Encode a NIfTI file into a compressed .jvol file.

jvol-rust encode [OPTIONS] <INPUT> <OUTPUT>

items.title: Arguments
items.text: Argument Description <INPUT> Input NIfTI file (.nii or .nii.gz) <OUTPUT> Output .jvol file path

items.title: Options
items.text: Option Default Description -q, --quality 60 JPEG quality (1–100, higher = better quality) -b, --block-size 8 Block size for 3D DCT -v, --verbose off Print encoding details and timing

items.title: Example
items.text:
jvol-rust encode brain.nii.gz brain.jvol --quality 80 --block-size 8 --verbose

Outputs the elapsed time in seconds to stdout (for scripting/benchmarking).

items.title: jvol-rust decode
items.text:
Decode a .jvol file back to NIfTI format.

jvol-rust decode [OPTIONS] <INPUT> <OUTPUT>

items.title: Arguments
items.text: Argument Description <INPUT> Input .jvol file path <OUTPUT> Output NIfTI file (.nii or .nii.gz)

items.title: Options
items.text: Option Default Description -v, --verbose off Print decoding details and timing

items.title: Example
items.text: jvol-rust decode brain.jvol brain_decoded.nii.gz --verbose

items.title: jvol-rust bench
items.text:
Benchmark the core encode and decode algorithms on a NIfTI file. This subcommand times only the core algorithm (no file I/O overhead), making it ideal for fair performance comparisons.

jvol-rust bench [OPTIONS] <INPUT>

items.title: Arguments
items.text: Argument Description <INPUT> Input NIfTI file (.nii or .nii.gz)

items.title: Options
items.text: Option Default Description -q, --quality 60 JPEG quality (1–100) -b, --block-size 8 Block size for 3D DCT

items.title: Example
items.text:
jvol-rust bench brain.nii.gz --quality 60 --block-size 8

Outputs two space-separated floats: encode_time decode_time (in seconds).

items.title: Pre-built binaries
items.text:
Download the latest release for your platform from the Releases page.

macOS (Apple Silicon)macOS (Intel)Linux (x86_64) 
curl -LO https://github.com/fepegar/jvol-rust/releases/latest/download/jvol-rust-aarch64-apple-darwin.tar.gz
tar xzf jvol-rust-aarch64-apple-darwin.tar.gz
sudo mv jvol-rust /usr/local/bin/

curl -LO https://github.com/fepegar/jvol-rust/releases/latest/download/jvol-rust-x86_64-apple-darwin.tar.gz
tar xzf jvol-rust-x86_64-apple-darwin.tar.gz
sudo mv jvol-rust /usr/local/bin/

curl -LO https://github.com/fepegar/jvol-rust/releases/latest/download/jvol-rust-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.tar.gz
tar xzf jvol-rust-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.tar.gz
sudo mv jvol-rust /usr/local/bin/

items.title: Building from source
items.text:
Requires Rust 1.80 or later.

git clone https://github.com/fepegar/jvol-rust.git
cd jvol-rust
cargo build --release

The binary will be at target/release/jvol-rust.

items.title: Encode a NIfTI volume
items.text: jvol-rust encode brain.nii.gz brain.jvol

items.title: Decode back to NIfTI
items.text: jvol-rust decode brain.jvol brain_decoded.nii.gz

items.title: Adjust quality
items.text:
Higher quality means better fidelity but larger file size (1–100, default: 60):

jvol-rust encode brain.nii.gz brain.jvol --quality 80

items.title: Adjust block size
items.text:
Larger blocks improve quality at the cost of compression ratio (default: 8):

jvol-rust encode brain.nii.gz brain.jvol --block-size 16

items.title: File format
items.text:
JVol Rust uses a custom binary format (.jvol):

  • The file is a gzip-compressed bincode archive
  • Contains: metadata (shape, affine, dtype, intercept, slope, block shape, quality), quantization table, and RLE-encoded DC/AC coefficient sequences

Note

The Rust .jvol format is not compatible with the Python jvol format (which uses NumPy .npz archives). Each implementation reads/writes its own format.
